hero.title: ⚡ Welcome to GitSimulator
hero.commit: Every commit is a checkpoint you can return to.
hero.branch: Every branch is a parallel universe you can experiment in.
hero.merge: Every merge brings parallel timelines together.

p1.part1: In 2005, Linus Torvalds — creator of Linux — spent
p1.part2: writing Git from scratch to replace the BitKeeper version control system. He needed something fast, distributed, and free. Today Git powers virtually all software development on Earth.

p1.part3: folder. This folder IS your entire repository — every commit, every branch, every piece of history lives here. If you delete

0.desc: Create new repo in a new folder
0.detail: Creates 'my-project/' folder AND initialises .git/ inside it
0.example: Like planting a seed in fresh soil 🌱

1.detail: Turns ANY existing folder into a git repo. Your files stay untouched.
1.example: Like installing a time machine into your house 🏠

2.desc: Copy a remote repo (with full history)
2.detail: Downloads the repo AND every commit ever made. You get the full time machine.
2.example: Like copying a book with every draft the author ever wrote 📓

3.detail: Only downloads the most recent state, not full history. Much faster for large repos.
3.example: Like reading today's newspaper, not the 30-year archive 📰

4.detail: Every commit is signed with your name and email. This is how blame/log shows who made changes.
4.example: Signing your name on everything you photograph 🖊️

5.detail: Must match your GitHub account email for commits to link to your profile.

body.intro: is the first file people read in a repository. It should quickly answer:
body.conclusion: A good README saves time for beginners, teammates, and future you.

p1.part1: In 2005, Linus Torvalds — creator of Linux — spent
p1.part2: writing Git from scratch to replace the BitKeeper version control system. He needed something fast, distributed, and free. Today Git powers virtually all software development on Earth.

p1.part3: folder. This folder IS your entire repository — every commit, every branch, every piece of history lives here. If you delete

0.desc: Create new repo in a new folder
0.detail: Creates 'my-project/' folder AND initialises .git/ inside it
0.example: Like planting a seed in fresh soil 🌱

1.detail: Turns ANY existing folder into a git repo. Your files stay untouched.
1.example: Like installing a time machine into your house 🏠

2.desc: Copy a remote repo (with full history)
2.detail: Downloads the repo AND every commit ever made. You get the full time machine.
2.example: Like copying a book with every draft the author ever wrote 📓

3.detail: Only downloads the most recent state, not full history. Much faster for large repos.
3.example: Like reading today's newspaper, not the 30-year archive 📰

4.example: Signing your name on everything you photograph 🖊️

5.detail: Must match your GitHub account email for commits to link to your profile.

p1.part2: : your Working Directory (files you're editing), the Staging Area (selected changes ready to snapshot), and the Repository (permanent commit history). git add moves files to staging. git commit snapshots staging into the repo.

grocery.title: The grocery store analogy
grocery.p1: Think of your project like a grocery store. All your files are items on the shelves, and whenever you make changes, it is like picking items you might want to buy. Staging in Git (git add) is like putting those items into your shopping cart. You can walk around and pick up many things, but only the items in your cart are actually ready to be purchased. When you run git commit, it is like going to the checkout and paying. You get a receipt that records exactly what was in your cart. So staging is simply choosing which changes you want to include in your next commit.

0.desc: See all changes in your working directory
0.detail: Shows 3 categories: Untracked (new files), Modified (changed files), Staged (ready to commit). Your project health checkup.
0.example: Looking in a mirror before leaving the house 🪞

1.detail: Only that file's changes go into the next commit. Useful for atomic commits.
1.example: Selecting one specific photo for the album 📷

2.desc: Stage ALL changes in current directory
2.detail: Every modified and new file goes to staging. Quick, but be careful you don't accidentally add secrets!

3.desc: Stage specific CHUNKS of a file
3.detail: Git shows each changed chunk and asks y/n. Lets you make surgical commits: 'only stage the bugfix, not the debug logs'.
3.example: Picking the exact best frame from a photo burst ✂️

4.detail: Creates an immutable commit object with: your changes, message, timestamp, author, and a pointer to the parent commit.
4.example: Taking the photograph and writing the caption on the back 📸

5.detail: Replace the last commit's message or add forgotten changes. Creates a NEW commit hash — don't use on pushed commits!
5.example: Correcting the caption on the photo you just took ✏️
5.warning: Never amend commits you've already pushed to a shared branch!

6.detail: --oneline=compact, --graph=ASCII branch lines, --all=show all branches. Your best friend for understanding history.
6.example: The family album with a family tree showing who came from whom 📖

7.detail: Red lines = removed, Green lines = added. Shows what's different between your working directory and the last commit.
7.example: Comparing two versions of a letter side by side 📄

8.desc: Show what IS staged (ready to commit)
8.detail: Same as diff but shows the staging area vs last commit. See exactly what your next commit will contain.

9.desc: Discard unstaged changes to a file
9.detail: ⚠️ Dangerous — this THROWS AWAY your changes! Use to revert a file to its last committed state.
9.example: Throwing away your photo and taking a fresh one 🗑️
9.warning: This permanently discards your local changes. Cannot be undone!

interactive.title: Interactive Commit Graph Simulator
interactive.line1: Type git commands below and watch the graph build in real-time.
interactive.line2: the graph updates instantly to show exactly what happens.
